column1: This is the current state workflow for performing eligibility checks for our RCM customers:
1. Offshore \*E Biller pulls a list of the customer's upcoming appointments for the next day
2. Offshore \*E Biller does the actual eligibility check in AMD and sends spreadsheet over to Elation

3. Internal \*E Biller goes into each individual patient and adds tags\. Internal \*E Biller takes 3\-5 minutes per patient to do the actual act of tagging because they have to login to the customer's Elation EHR instance
column2: open each individual patient chart
column3: and manually type in and add the patient tags\. Billers are spending 4\-6 hours per day across the current volume of 200\-250 patients \(as of mid\-May\)\.

column1: Here is a [Gong recording](https://app.gong.io/call?id=8063643608607102948) of the workflow\.
We need to reduce and ultimately eliminate the time spent in step 3 \- getting the eligibility data back into Elation\. This should ultimately be resolved by realtime eligibility \(RTE\) in Elation
column2: but we're multiple months away from RTE
column3: so we need a stop\-gap solution in the meantime to augment our Internal \*E Billers\.

column1: - We cannot meet RCM demand because we do not have enough \*E Biller capacity\. This step in the workflow is currently taking up almost a full FTE \(4\-6 hours\) who could be spending this time on other parts of the RCM workflow and better customer care\.
- Though not a top ask from Tier 2
column2: a bulk tagging capability would also be helpful to address customer issues around patient tags\.

column1: A key TF product objective in H2’21 is to drive business viability by reducing admin and operational costs at scale\.

TF customers leverage mid and low-level clinical staff operating at the top of their licensure as delegates to augment providers\. This augmentation creates capacity for providers \(most expensive resources\) to focus on the most critical aspects of care
column2: while clinical staff focuses on other tasks\. Optimizing the provider mix in this way enables these organizations to drive down their operating costs and consequently

column1: Making it seamless to manage delegates at scale better positions Elation to be an effective partner to TF customers and reach our company objective of being the market leader for tech forwards\.
TF customers face operational challenges with the delegate experience in Elation\. Any change to a provider's delegates in Elation today requires the provider to navigate to their settings
column2: and manually configure those changes\.

column1: This is problematic for organizations because:
1. providers do not reliably make these changes promptly
column2: impacting clinical staff's ability to augment the provider and

column1: 2. managing delegates is a high-volume/high-frequency task that becomes time-consuming for and bottlenecked by providers as organizations scale rapidly\.
\{can we look at delegate growth by customer year over year?\}\.

Establishing a mechanism for administrators at TF organizations to manage delegate settings on behalf of providers would allow them to be more responsive to changes in the organization and effectively leverage mid and low-level staff to augment providers\. A hypothesis is that we would see smaller
column2: more frequent delegate changes instead of the bulk changes that happen today \(owned by \*E operationally\)\.

column1: Adding the ability to set a user group as a provider's delegate would make the process seamless by limiting the number of places an administrator would need to touch in order to completely deploy a delegate\.
1. Admin-managed delegation is a key requirement for Teladoc's ability to scale its operations in 2H'21\.
2. Admin-managed billing delegates is a contractual obligation to Cityblock to be delivered in Q3'21\.

column1: Preventing non-admin users from making changes to settings that impact the entire CDU will reduce the time spent by administrators managing/correcting these settings.

Implicit in this hypothesis is the assumption that these organizations set the requirements for these settings and desire to enforce these requirements at the CDU level instead of allowing users to set/override the preferences individually.
**Things to consider for validation -- add to data section later**
1. How often have non-admin users made changes to `Visit Note Templates`?
2. How often have non-admin users made changes to `Practice Settings`?

6. We need to consider how this will translate to SMB customers who often do not have the same administrative functions as Enterprise customers. The recommendation is to re-use the existing `Admin Only` toggle
column2: which gives the organization the option to choose a more admin-managed approach (TF/Ent) or a less rigid approach (SMB).

8362122;f6e49ed9-7b2b-456b-a1ce-7629bf878ca2;Mgt Org Settings - User identity proofing status and credentials are propagated x-CDU;# 1.1 Strategic Alignment
x-CDU providers must go through the ID proofing and credentialing process in each additional CDU they work from
column2: even though they have previously completed this step in another CDU within a management organization.

column1: The ID proofing and credentialing steps are critical to a provider's ability to care for patients in a CDU
column2: as it is required for the provider to eRX. When providers fail to complete this process in a new CDU
column3: they enter a failure state where they cannot take desired actions at the point of care.

column2: there are no technical or compliance reasons why a provider would need to complete this process multiple times within the same organization.

column1: Propagating provider's ID proofing status and credentials x-CDU will make the onboarding process more seamless for x-CDU providers by eliminating duplicate onboarding steps.
It can take up to 10 mins to complete the ID proofing and credentialing process through Experian. This time cost grows linearly with the number of CDUs a provider works in (linked accounts). So far
column2: we have seen providers who work from up to 14 CDUs (~140 mins if they needed to complete this per CDU). Solving this would keep the time cost relatively constant to ~10mins even as more CDUs are added.

column1: There are 2 different IDProofing workflows in Elation:
The EPCS workflow lives within MDToolbox
column2: and we'd need to carefully evaluate the impact of changing the IDProofing paradigm for this workflow more broadly before changing it. As such
column3: the EPCS workflow will be out of scope for the first iteration of this work.

column1: **TF Objective: Be the Market Leader for Tech Forwards**
This opportunity should expand the utility of Elation APIs for Tech Forward cohort.

Tech forward customers that have built companion software that require patient context to drive operational workflows. A component of the patient context is the Patient History section of the clinical profile in the Elation EHR. Currently customers do  not have ability to access this information without logging into the app and manually copy pasting into the external system. Addressing this should reduce overhead and data integrity issues with manual copy/ paste.
This API is prioritized in anticipation for OakStreet contract execution and estimated low lift to build and support.
- Revenue - Low impact to drive revenue growth.
- Churn - High impact due to commitment to OakStreet.

column2: majority of clinical users view this information in Elation EHR.

8512669;6c2d0a8b-99e9-4514-8d78-9fab718f7a76;State EPCS: state-specific controlled vs non-controlled rules;This enables multi-state eRx support for drugs with different controlled substance status across states.
High-level story: Practices with multiple locations can ePrescribe gabapentin and other meds with state-specific non-controlled vs controlled rules.
Some drugs have a different controlled substance status across states. For example
column2: gabapentin is a controlled substance in KY but not in NY. What this means functionally in Elation is that clinicians located in NY cannot prescribe gabapentin to patients in KY. Why? Because the controlled substance eRx form is completely separate from the regular
column4: and the drug search results in either form conform to the controlled substance status based on the clinician's state
column5: not the pharmacy's state. This means that the NY clinician will not be able to select gabapentin from the controlled substance form since it's not a controlled substance in NY.

column1: Idea is that combining the controlled and non-controlled substances eRx forms will resolve this issue.
Some inputs that would be helpful:
- how many drugs fall into the same category as gabapentin
column2: where the controlled substance status is different across state lines?
how many times has a XO provider prescribed gabapentin in the past X months?

column2: scheduling information in Elation is based on the time zone associated with the practice\. This can create confusion in cases where the patient is not in the practice time zone\.

we plan to convert available appointments on the booking site into the patient's time zone \(pulled from their browser\) to more accurately represent appointment availability\. column2: otherwise fall back to the practice time zone

14604833;64e3d71c-bf20-41c1-a6f4-45354788a908;Credential Status Visibility;"Credentialing refers to the process by which providers gain prior approval to render a set of covered services from a payer based on their training and facilities in which they plan to render services. This agreement between the payer and the provider includes a list of covered services and negotiated rates in the form of a contract
column2: so this process is sometimes referred to as contracting. After a provider is credentialed and a contract is established between the practice and the payer
column3: the practice can start submitting claims with dates of service after their credential start date. If practices submit claims with dates of service prior to being credentialed
column4: they run the risk of not being reimbursed. Sometimes providers can get retroactive credit for services rendered prior to their credential date

column1: Credentialing and enrollment are often referred to as just ""credentialing."" Enrollment refers to the process by which a practice signs up for electronic claim submission
column2: eligibility
column4: and ERA receipts. Enrollment is an agreement between the provider and the clearinghouse
column5: sometimes facilitated by the billing system
column6: while credentialing and contracting are agreements between the payer and the provider. If a practice attempts to submit claims electronically prior to enrollment
column7: the claims will be rejected by the clearinghouse and not paid.

column1: In order to help practices avoid lost revenue due to lack of credentialing approval and/or avoid submission delays due to lack of enrollments
column2: it may be helpful to track this information in Elation and use it to alert staff and physicians if they render a service to a patient covered by an entity where the physician is not credentialed or enrolled. While the system of record for this information should be the billing system
column3: this information should still be visible to staff and providers to help avoid lost revenue and/or alert staff to what urgent steps are necessary to capture future revenue.";feature;Backlog;smokeLight

column1: 14604835;50b3a5a5-bdd3-445e-88b1-d6936f00ae10;Post adjudication claims re-submission;"For: Coding Revision Integration
In both FFS and VBC arrangements
column2: claims are submitted to payers to substantiate services rendered. These claims are submitted to payers via clearinghouses (electronic) or directly
column5: or denied. Practices closely track rejections and denials to understand why the claims were not paid
column6: then take necessary action and re-submit the claim for reimbursement
column7: or else practices run the risk of not being reimbursed for services rendered and lose out on that potential revenue.

column1: The changes required for resubmission for rejections and denials range from small tweaks to demographics or insurance information to re-coding the claim. For changes that need to be made by the physician and/or in the context of clinical information contained in the visit note
column2: ideally these would be made in Elation in order to keep clinical personas in the EHR (aka in Elation). Resubmissions are not limited to rejections and denials however; it is common for VBO's to make changes to capitated claims that have already been approved in order to most accurately capture patient outcomes
column3: usually in the form of RAF scores for the panel. While ideally these coding gaps or issues would be able to be anticipated prior to submission
column4: we want to provide a robust billing system experience for practices that at least minimally supports unhappy paths.

column1: 14604926;ca4be985-5102-402a-a4f2-dbf409f48bd8;Outstanding Balance Visibility;"In both FFS and VBC reimbursement arrangements with payers
column2: patients still often bear some financial responsibility (payers refer to this as ""cost sharing"") to the practice for services rendered. Sometimes patients may elect to not bill through insurance and choose to pay directly
column3: sometimes referred to as self pay. While practices may try to collect copays or self-pay charges up front
column4: often times patients carry a balance after the visit - especially if they owe a deductible or coinsurance
column5: or if they weren't able to pay up front. It is easiest to collect from patients while they are physically in the office or while the practice may have them on the phone for another reason.

column1: Front office staff are usually the main touchpoint for patients in a practice
column2: so it would be useful for these staff members to have visibility into any outstanding balance a patient has in order to collect in a timely manner. In order to complete this task
column3: the staff must also have the ability to answer basic questions the patient may have about how that outstanding balance was calculated to avoid follow ups after the patient is not as engaged or motivated to pay the charges.

column1: Note - qualitative feedback has been positive as this was received well with RCM practices when Collectly integration was rolled out. Quantitative feedback below:
1. Practices have begun collecting outstanding balances at the time of service. **18% of all outstanding balance payments were made via iFrame**
column2: and 55% of all payments collected were via iFrame experience (including copays). These %s are by total revenue

14605003;dc51571b-7cc0-459d-9fa0-c933f8526ba8;Claim Splitting;For VBC practices to maximize reimbursement
column2: it is essential that patients' RAF profile is a complete picture of their health\. In capitated arrangements
column3: each visit is still coded and submitted to payers to track patient health and interventions over time; these claims are not reimbursed per claim but instead are used to calculate regular payments \- usually per patient per month \- based on risk factors and improved health\.

column1: For sicker patients with more comorbidities
column2: this can often result in dozens of diagnosis codes on multiple claims\. X12 standards for 837 transactions \(electronic claim submission\)
column3: which have been widely adopted by most billing systems
column4: limits the maximum number of unique diagnosis codes per claim to 12
column5: which can lead to additional diagnoses not being captured and thus lost revenue\.

column1: In order to still capture the full picture of these patients' health in the form of complete diagnostic codes on every claim submitted to payers
column2: many practices and payers have adopted the practice of splitting claims\. These split claims have the same procedure codes for the patient
column5: and date of service but will contain different diagnostic codes on a subsequent claim in order to submit more than the maximum of 12 dx codes\.

column2: Elation should facilitate this process of claim splitting in some form or fashion when releasing bills to the billing system \(EHR only\) or to the clearinghouse directly \(AIO\)\.

Deep linking and SSO support for encounters (aka superbills) allows staff and providers to cross reference information at the encounter/bill level between the systems to support claim edits
column5: and other important tasks without the need to log in to another system and search for the same record. ";feature;Backlog;smokeLight

- Users do not have a designed structured workflow to document injections administered in-house or at other practices\. We understand users are using the Document Vaccination form
column2: prescription form
column4: or other free text fields or spaces to note injections

column1: - The Document Vaccination form has some fields that are useful for injections
column2: such as the administrate site
column5: but the form is vaccine-focused and clutters the vaccination section in the Clinical Profile\. If the practice has an immunization registry interface
column6: the Document Vaccination form is automatically pre-selected to be submitted to a registry and the user must take steps to change destination to not submit to the form\. Additionally
column7: injected medications should be visible in medication views and considered for drug-drug interactions\.

column1: - The Prescription Form is an order form
column2: not geared toward in-house administration and documentation
column3: and is missing injection-specific fields like site
column5: and lot number\. Using the Prescription Form for injection documentation
column6: there are helpful benefits such as injected medications appearing in the medication history and drug-drug and drug-allergy checking
column7: but users may forget the aforementioned details that are required for billing and reimbursement\.

column1: - Visit notes and other freetext spaces in Elation allow for flexibility but do not allow for structured data capture\. Additionally
column2: users may forget to include required fields
column3: so many users have created visit note templates to encourage capture of required fields\.

column1: - Coding automation for injections cannot be successfully built and implemented without sufficient injection documentation\.

column2: structured form to document injections administered in house\. All users
column4: will be able to document injections\.

column1: - Injections are a core aspect of primary care and lack of a consistent workflow is problematic for Elation's core users\.

column2: this has been an escalated issue for strategic customers like ASAS and Medici Health by Design
column3: and as such there have been commitments to deliver this functionality in Q4 2024

Revise the patient payment collection flow to unify the experience across the AIO platform and maximize the revenue stream opportunity\.  The key elements for the first iteration here include:
- Patient balance display and links to Elation Billing patient pages
- Updated payment processing dialogue across both EHR and PM
column2: which can differentiate copay vs outstanding balance payments

- Revise Thor email links and checkout page to use Stripe connected acct resources

**Why Now?** Patient payment flows are an integral part of practice operations and the current experience is not consistent with customer expectations of an AIO solution\.  The current workarounds are clunky and leave too much room for user error and we believe our solutions will increase customer satisfaction and efficiency\.

17365529;3a4cab2d-3192-4929-bde7-458cd5271939;Upgrade to Kubernetes 1.23;- Upgrade our clusters to Kubernetes 1\.23
Amazon EKS now supports Kubernetes version 1\.23\. If you are running stateful workloads on your EKS cluster using Amazon EBS volumes
column2: then you must install the EBS CSI driver before upgrading to EKS version 1\.23 to avoid any interruption to your workloads\.

column1: Kubernetes clusters running on AWS support provisioning and mounting Amazon EBS volumes to pods\. In EKS version 1\.22 or earlier
column2: there are two ways to perform this task\. You can use the in\-tree storage plugin \(provisioner of type [kubernetes\.io/aws\-ebs](http://kubernetes.io/aws-ebs)\) or the Amazon EBS CSI driver \(provisioner of type [ebs\.csi\.aws\.com](http://ebs.csi.aws.com)\) \[1\]\. In\-tree storage plugins are deprecated in Kubernetes\. In line with this deprecation
column3: EBS in\-tree plugin will be deprecated in Amazon EKS clusters version 1\.23 and later
column4: and its functionality will be performed by the EBS CSI driver\.

column1: To make the transition from in\-tree plugins to CSI drivers smoother
column2: the Kubernetes project created the CSI migration feature \[2\]\. This feature translates in\-tree APIs to equivalent CSI APIs and delegates operations to a replacement CSI driver\. With this feature
column3: you shouldn’t notice a difference when provisioning and mounting Amazon EBS volumes to pods\. Existing StorageClass
column5: and PersistentVolumeClaim objects belonging to workloads continue to work\. Behind the scenes
column6: Kubernetes automatically delegates all storage management operations from in\-tree plugins to CSI drivers\. Amazon EKS has enabled the EBS CSI migration feature in EKS version 1\.23\. This means that management operations of storage objects that were previously backed by the in\-tree plugin will be handled by the EBS CSI driver\. The driver must be installed in your cluster for this seamless transition to work\.

column1: If you are using Amazon EBS volumes in your cluster
column2: then you must install the EBS CSI driver before upgrading to Amazon EKS version 1\.23 to avoid any interruption to your workloads\. EKS offers EBS CSI driver as an EKS add\-on to make it easier for you to adopt the driver\. You can find details on EBS CSI Migration in the EKS documentation \[3\] and instructions for installing the EBS CSI driver in the available User Guide \[4\]\.

column1: If you have any questions or concerns
column2: please reach out to AWS Support \[5\]\.

column2: the process of creating these accounts can be made very complicated when a customer is taking a 2nd look at Elation
column3: or if they are currently a patient of another Elation customer\. In these cases
column4: we often have to ask the customer to create another email address as we cannot re\-use the same email address\. This also contributes to the creation of duplicate trial accounts\.

column1: We also run into issues if the wrong NPI is entered when the trial account is created\. The email associated with the NPI for the trial account is often captured very early in the sales cycle and usually its an influencer within the practice vetting out Elation\. Therefore
column2: from signing with Elation to that very first interaction with our sales team
column3: which is usually weeks or months in between

column8: etc\. This overall creates confusion downstream
column9: such as the account having multiple trial accounts
column10: or the trial account being tied to an office manager's email who was originally the influencer that was vetting out Elation with our sales team\.

column1: We can avoid a lot of these issues by letting test accounts just be test accounts\. Instead of using them to create the customer's Elation instance
column2: we can just spin up a new instance when the customer is ready to move forward\. We tell customers to not enter any PHI or real data in the test account anyway
column3: so no real data is lost if this test account is deleted\. We could create automation to spin up a new instance for the customer with a single button click
column4: and send them an invitation to access it \(like spinning up an Ellie branch\)\.

column1: This may also help with our goal of reducing 'not right fit' customers
column2: as easier access to a trial account may help customers more quickly realize if Elation is missing key features they need\. This will help reduce the time spent onboarding customers who might end up churning for product reasons\.

17970480;481f0b6e-17c2-42ed-bab5-318c579fe288;Automate retries when fax fails to send because of Softlinx Connection Error;"*STORY*

column2: I want my failed faxes to automatically retry if they likely failed due to a transient ‘internal error’ so that I can improve the likelihood that my fax sends properly with minimal delay or effort on my part\.

column1: - Per Josh: In looking into the fax errors
column2: I found the most common cause of support cases is actually ""Internal Error""
column3: which occurs when we have a letter ID but no fax ID \(meaning we failed to deliver the fax to Softlinx in the first place\)\. I suspect the vast majority of those occur when Softlinx has random connection timeouts \([which happens >100 times a day for fax sends](https://graylog.el8.io/search?q=%22Error+while+attempting+to+send+a+fax%22+AND+ConnectionError&rangetype=relative&relative=172800)\)\. I don't think it would make sense to enable retries for the entire integration \(since in most cases we just poll again in a few minutes and it's fine; fax sends are a bit of a special case\)
column4: some local retry logic around sends would likely reduce those failure significantly

column1: - Fax errors that were the result of connection issues

column2: which indicates exactly what the expectation is from a FHIR standpoint\.

column1: > The NPI is a 10\-digit number that will be used to identify you to your health care partners
column2: including all payers
column3: in all HIPAA standard transactions\. The NPI will replace the identifiers you currently use in HIPAA standard transactions that you conduct with Medicare and with other health plans\. You will need an NPI prior to enrolling with Medicare\. There are two types of health care providers in terms of NPIs:

column1: > - Type 1 — Health care providers who are individuals
column2: including physicians
column4: and all sole proprietors\. An individual is eligible for only one NPI\.

column1: > <span data-color=""#36b37e"">\*\*\* Type 2 — Health care providers who are organizations
column2: including physician groups
column5: and the corporation formed when an individual incorporates him/herself\.\*\*

column2: in order to satisfy this requirement

column2: or leverage an existing field in the data model\.

column1: 2. Add the Type 2 NPI concept into the API v2 model for `Practice` entity and corresponding serializer\(s\)\.
3. Add the ability \(e\.g\. in EL8 admin
column2: Practice Settings
column3: something else\) for a given Practice to save their Type 2 NPI\.

column1: 4. Hook up CDC in Debezium if the field will be stored on a table which doesn’t already have a listener\.

18815549;5973a194-81ec-476d-b3a7-561b9b328eeb;*ERA Autopost Fixes;**Update:** ERA autopost work was descoped and limited to a high\-impact story to reduce 'cannot autopost' errors for users \(THOR\-334\)\.  The remainder of the scope will be addressed with work on Denial Management features Q1 2024
**Problem**:  There are ERAs that return the error ""cannot auto post"" to the user
column2: which creates a lot of manual work\.  Current autopost also posts the denials as written in the ERA\. Most customers don't want to accept this outcome and will correct and resubmit or appeal the decisions\. There is manual work created to delete those postings\.  If there is a need to add any claims manually
column3: you can only do so by superbill number\.

column1: **Solution**: These enhancements will give the option to withhold the denial postings as well as give preview insight as to how many denials on an ERA etc\.   In addition
column2: when manually posting
column3: we will provide an option to search by patient when adding claims\. Some additional search filters for the ERA table will be added \(e\.g\. date filters\)

column1: **Why Now?** These auto\-post updates are table stakes for users who don't want any denials to be automatically written off and searching by patient is in response to multiple user requests \(not all reflected in PB\)\.

**Problem**: Carrier Lists in the EHR \(non AIO customers\) are often a mess \(100s\+ and full of duplicates\)\. Implementation and onboarding works with the customer to clean up their list prior to go live \(using existing EHR merge insurance functionality\)
column2: which includes mapping the list to Thor carriers\.

the customer will no longer manage the carrier list in the EHR and will manage in the PM \(Thor\) by adding to their favorites list\.  We will also display favorites at the top of the list in the patient demographic search area\.

column1: **Why Now?**  Insurance carrier list management and the integration process are AIO table stakes
You can see the new EHR view design evolving in Figma as well \(WIP\)
- Decouple the zus and Elation experiences to allow for better A/B testing with interested customers
- Get an understanding of Zus' process of ensuring they are being recorded as an OBO \(On Behalf Of\) entity \- see slack

column3: there’s currently no serializer to represent this
column4: though we do have a serializer for the `ClinicalForm` object \(see `ClinicalQuestionnaireSerializer`\)\. The ask would be to have these form collections flow into the event stream\.

19897621;ecdcd432-8846-481b-8cad-4a440ad63ebe;Infrastructure Update: Crosswalk API Enhancements;**Problem**: The crosswalk database and application which 'connects' the EHR and PMS doesn't have adequate tooling to handle troubleshooting and debugging when needed.

**Solution**: Migrate the crosswalk away from the current REDIS instance to our own RDS tables. The codebase for the crosswalk will also be migrated to our centralized source code enabling easier debugging and code revisions (and versioning) going forward.

**Why Now?** The app code for the crosswalk is also the only part of app code not deployed via a versioning system (GitHub / CodePipeline). Making this move now helps advance our compliance efforts as well simplifies the transition prior to scale and enables us to better debug and iterate this service as we scale into general availability (GA).

column2: each DX code will have it's own description displayed instead of the typical combined description\.

column1: - Split of Dx’s in Problem List
Suvida is a reference customer we want to keep happy
- When a depression screening questionnaire is taken by the provider and exported to the visit note
column2: Elation automates the addition of the procedural code to the visit note and bill
column3: but the user must still manually add the diagnostic code  z13\.31 for ""encounter included a depression screening""\.  This dx code is useful to report for every depression screening regardless of the outcome because its solely associated with the fact that the screening took place
column4: as opposed to typical diagnostic codes that confirm an actual diagnosis\. Because this code is reported for  every encounter that includes a depression screening
column5: the provider is having to manually add this dx code every time a depression screening questionnaire is exported to the visit note\.

column1: - If this same dx code is added every time the depression screening cpt automation is triggered regardless of questionnaire outcome
column2: we can bundle these codes together in our automation

column1: - For the utmost flexibility allow users to identify a Dx code that will be automated to the bill when a depression screening questionnaire is taken and exported to the visit note\. This will allow practices to easily react to coding protocol changes \(ex: z13\.31 becomes a deprecated code and a new one takes its place to represent depression screening encounter\)
- Furthers our goal of coding automation to eliminate manual intervention/ result in fewer clicks

column2: it was also flagged that the current Audit log process in Hippo is very load-intensive and shouldn’t be expanded further

column1: - B-Team therefore partnered with External Platform to set up AuditLog pipeline from Event Streaming
column2: decoupled from Hippo

column1: - The Audit processor has been running for the last month in Production for 10 RCM practices
- Data Platform has migrated Hippo historical data to Snowflake
column2: only for the 10 RCM practices

column1: - This is a heavy process and we should try to only run it once up to a cutover date/time TBD
- We now need to figure out how to completely move away from the Hippo legacy audit log tracking to get the database scalability benefits\.
## What are the gaps before we can move the whole Audit Log process to Event Streaming and deprecate the Hippo legacy process?
- The External Platform team must enable Event Streaming for ALL practices in Production
- It is currently only enabled for 10 practices\. It will take at least a month to feel comfortable with enabling all practices
- The Data Platform team must run the whole data extraction process from Hippo data to Snowflake
- They also need to figure out how to align certain fields \(e\.g\. Table names\)
- The ACES team must update the ‘Patient Lookup’ Admin tool to point to  Snowflake\.
- The 3 teams involved feel comfortable about a Q3 timeline for this outcome\.

21836375;7abe6b93-4cc4-4598-bd32-a3050d3b29dc;allow users to modify visit note timer;**Problem Statement**

- Practices are not able to adjust the visit note timer which becomes an issue in cases where the provider left the note open by accident while the timer continues to run\. Currently the timer can be started/stopped but not modified\. If it could be modified it'd unlock useful workflows for practices that require recording time spent with patients
- Clinicians have reached out asking to turn the timer off because they do not want the incorrect time associated with their note documentation\. In addition to accidentally leaving the note open
column2: if they stop and forget to restart

column1: - Currently for programs like CCM which require time tracking for both documentation and billing purposes
column2: we do not have a way to record time spent on activities in a reportable fashion\. Groups have resorted in some cases to creating non-visit notes with specific text formatting so that manually typed time spent can be extracted on the backend or manually compiled -- a non-scalable process\.

column1: - Per Phil this has been steadily requested by multiple practices for quite some time
- Note that the timer's functionality can be disabled via feature flag `NoVisitNoteTimer`
column2: which happens to be set in some of our baseline test practices so just a heads up that you’ll need to flip that to see the experience\.

column1: - There’s not a good reason that I’m aware of that the times can be started/stopped but not edited\. Just a lack of UX for it\.

- The [API](https://github.com/elationemr/hippo/blob/feature/nabla/elation/notes/views.py#L1311) supports sending up values\. I think it may ignore updates to timers that are stopped so it may need a slight change in order to support this but otherwise I think the change would be entirely on the frontend UX to support correcting a mistake and adjusting the time\.

column1: However we do not currently have the ability to do filtering on properties of these document types\. There is a broader goal to add more document field level filtering to these `PatientItem`s\. However here we want to focus on adding the ability to filter by `Visitnote.type`
This will involve work in 4 areas\.
1. The chronological record search functions via ElasticSearch\. Thus we will need to add the type value to `VisitNoteDocument` which is what represents visit note in ES\.
2. Updating `/patient/<patient_id>/search-chart-feed/` endpoint to accept & use params to filter by visit\_note\_types
1. update the view to accept the query params
2. `update search_chart_feed_index` to accept the parameter for VisitNote type filtering
3. update `_search_elasticsearch` to be able to do the filtering\.
3. Create a new detail endpoint to show list the relevant Note Types for a given patient
2. So we only fill the UI with types of notes the patient has\.
1. design and code the display components for the `VisitNoteType` Filter
2. use `/practice//visit-note-types/` to fill out options for the filter\.
3. Pass the `VisitNoteType.ids` to filter to the `search_chart_feed` endpoint\.
5. Reindexing all `VisitNoteDocuments` as to populate the new `type` field\.

23922990;c4c628aa-04c1-4cc5-8bb9-4e7d612ab2e4;Microservice public APIv2 endpoints;## Problem Statement
- When exposing a service as an APIv2 endpoint
column2: the current mechanism is to making it available through Hippo first\.

column1: - This is not ideal for many reasons:
- The whole point of a distributed architecture is to be able to deploy continuously independently from other teams but having to make a change in hippo defeats this purpose entirely\.
- Having an extra layer before the service introduced potential resiliency issues \(chained calls\)
column2: and additional latency\. Since all calls flow through Hippo also doesn’t remove any load pressure from this latter\.

column1: We therefore would need to come up with a design that allows routing certain endpoints to their underlying service\.
- \*\* routing: the current APIv2 base route is `/api/2.0`\.
- We would need to route a particular resource to its appropriate service

- security: the current APIv2 supports OAUTH2/OIDC authN mechanism\. The same mechanism should secure the microservices exposed publicly through APIv2\. This concern should be covered by [https://elationhealth\.atlassian\.net/wiki/spaces/DEV/pages/2183987362/ADR002+\~\~+Ability+to+invoke+Hippo+APIv2+from+internal+services](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DEV/pages/2183987362/ADR002+~~+Ability+to+invoke+Hippo+APIv2+from+internal+services%7Csmart-link)
- Multiple teams need to expose APIv2 endpoints but have microservices
- increase count of endpoints that exist and are leverage w/o Hippo dependency
- time saved by avoiding Hippo tax

column2: we need to enable 2-way websocket (WSS) communication to/from microservices. The use case is currently audio files.

column1: The [current WSS solution ](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DEV/pages/401080352/Websockets+Service)only supports 1-way communication and is not something we want to invest in much further.
The goal of this epic is proposing a general solution for this problem that can be generalized in the future.
* The AI Scribe solution is able to leverage this solution for their GA in Q1 '24
* The solution offers an authentication mechanism as secure as regular access to Hippo backend/microservices gateway
* The solution can be generalized to other microservices without full rewrite (ideally just configuration)";feature;Cancelled;red
24691253;87823862-af2a-4732-bd53-ef0b025ea7ec;AI-based Document analysis spike;"There are a number of attractive features related to analyzing documents
column2: extracting named entities
column4: etc\. The most prevalent gets referred to as “Document Management” internally at Elation and in some of the industry\. It basically means automating the very laborious work that goes into managing a practice’s inbound fax queue\. Faxes need to be classified
column5: the patient needs to be identified\. Sometimes pages need to be rotated
column6: or grouped into multiple individual documents\.

column1: We’ve done some spikes in past years looking at the quality of OCR technology and strategies for predicting patients or document types/contents\. I’d like to see us investigate with fresh eyes given how much advancement there’s been in AI/ML since\. In my own small experiments GPT\-4 does a pretty incredible job at classification and named entity extraction whether from OCR’d text or using vision directly in the new multi\-modal model\. I’m pretty confident we could build these features fairly quickly using the most capable available LLMs
column2: but I’m less certain about the cost or performance implications\.

column1: I’d love for a spike to provide us some high level architectural options and napkin math\. The output would be used to help the application development team make 2024 roadmap decisions by providing a high level understanding of some of the costs/tradeoffs/feasibility for features that involve analysis of documents/images\.
- ADR/Technical Documentation that outlines suggested approach\(es\) and trade\-offs as well as calling out pitfalls and directions we’d advise against
- Per\-page cost projections for evaluated solutions
- Outputs are used to make > 1 product decision contributing to 2024 roadmap success and saving app\-dev teams time
- Increased AI fluency on Eng Enablement team

24691278;b27b30fe-ca7e-4b19-909e-1a41c779e82b;AI: Make it as easy as possible to wire up OpenAI Assistants to Slack;OpenAI has released an [Assistants API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/overview) which makes it very easy to build a chatbot that can do all of the ChatGPT stuff as well as support retrieval/RAG for answering questions about uploaded files/documents
column2: write and run code in a container
column3: or return structured function signatures that can be wired up to internal systems/actions.

column1: I’ve built one that we’ll very likely be wiring into Ellie when Cory picks that work up in December
column2: but in the meantime I’m seeing teams starting to find loads of other use cases for them as well. Ted built one that has a copy of Elation’s style guide and can evaluate text or images and give feedback about how well the copy complies along with suggestions for improvement. Conan and Tier 3 are interested in bots that understand our schema and can help design queries for analytics or troubleshooting (I have a [video](https://www.loom.com/share/d039eb0dda2e496b896533b6bf177236) that gives you some idea how this might work).

column1: The thing the Assistants API doesn’t give us is a UX for interacting with these things (other than the OpenAI Playground). Being able to pull them into Slack with relatively low friction I think would be a really valuable unlock – hopefully in total productivity
column2: but also in helping build the broader team’s familiarity and intuition about AI and what LLMs are capable of.

column1: 4. **Appearance of Cognitive Status \(latest form collection\) items in Cognitive Status block**
1. GIVEN: A provider is documenting a patient’s Cognitive Status in Note 2\.0
2. WHEN: The provider is viewing the latest form collection for a form
3. THEN: The form collection is represented by a clickable blue link
4. AND THEN: The link can open the Clinical Form Dialog to view the collected form questions and responses\.
5. **Appearance of Cognitive Status \(past form collection\) items in Cognitive Status block**
1. GIVEN: A provider is documenting a patient’s Cognitive Status in Note 2\.0
2. WHEN: The provider is viewing the latest form collection for a form
3. THEN: The form collection is represented by a muted grey link w/ strikethrough
4. AND THEN: The link may open the Clinical Form Dialog to view the collected form questions and responses \(data may or may not be in cache and accessible after page refresh\)\.
6. **Sync Cognitive ClinicalFormCollections to Clinical Profile and Cognitive Status block on Save**
1. GIVEN: A provider is capturing responses in the Clinical Form Dialog
2. AND GIVEN: A provider is documenting a patient’s Cognitive Status in Note 2\.0 \(block is present\)
3. WHEN: The provider saves the clinical form dialog
4. THEN: A new form collection for the form is saved/created for the patient
5. AND THEN: The latest form collection for the form is updated in LHS Clinical Profile
6. AND THEN: The latest form collection is appended as an item/link to the Cognitive Status block\.

25409909;d2ec3b02-3169-49ce-8145-ff59bb8ae5f5;Note Assist: Draft Prescription orders on behalf of providers;[PRD](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/PROD/pages/2685043847/PRD+-+AI+Actions+wip)
Framing: AI Actions enables us to achieve the dream of eliminating the administrative burden of documentation for providers\. With AI Actions
column2: providers focus on making clinical decisions
column3: while the system intelligently carries out the necessary steps to get the clinical decision in a state where it can be executed\. The provider then reviews and signs off on the draft\.

column1: AI Actions presents an opportunity to significantly reduce the time providers spend on administrative steps required to capture their clinical intent and execute on the task by offloading the order drafting \(related functional job\) to an LLM during a patient\-provider encounter \(core functional job\)\.
1. Provider makes clinical decision to prescribe a specific medication for a patient during an encounter

column2: a new and improved documentation experience on Elation\!

column1: Note 2 represents a fundamental rethinking and evolution of the documentation experience
column2: prioritizing flexibility for clinicians at the point of care while providing the structure and guidance that value\-based care organizations need to succeed at scale\.

column1: Building on a decade of learnings
column2: Note 2 advances the clinical\-first ease of use that clinicians have come to love from Elation
column3: introducing a dynamic rich text editor
column4: powered by blocks that contextually adapt to the documentation needs of providers and practices\.

column2: the documentation experience evolves in three key ways:

column1: - It transitions *from a fixed form to a dynamic canvas* that can be customized to the unique documentation needs of each provider and organization\.
- It *replaces strict fields with dynamic blocks*
column2: enabling clinicians to compose their notes to best represent the complexity and nuances of a patient's health\.
it *advances from linear computation to atomic computation*
column3: empowering organizations to support providers in critical moments when they are making decisions about a patient's care\.

column1: **Hypothesis for this scope of work:** We hypothesize that starting with a functional version of VN2
column2: enabling providers to see patients in production

As part of Hippo Daily Deployment efforts
column2: we need to make sure Hippo Long running tasks are not impacted by deployments\.

column1: Certain operations can take many hours and sometimes days\.

column2: these tasks would get interrupted and potentially lost completely\.

column1: The goal of this epic is to replace the mechanism for running these tasks so if Hippo code gets updated
column2: they will still complete\. This approach should be ok as even if the database schema gets updated
column3: the changes will be backwards compatible and these the older code will still be able to proceed\.

column1: 1. Celery Tasks longer than 5 minutes are run using the new mechanism and continue running even when code is deployed
2. The new mechanism offers enough observability to track what tasks are running
column2: which are completed and which are failed\.

column1: 3. The new mechanism offers ability to alert if a task fails\.
4. The new mechanism offers retry capabilities
5. The new mechanism is generic enough and extensible enough to handle other use cases \(e\.g\. long running management commands

column4: which can lead to data loss and administrative errors\. This results in a messy and inefficient process
column5: causing operational friction for practices trying to maintain accurate insurance records\.

column1: The **Merge Insurance Carriers** feature allows practices to consolidate duplicate insurance carriers by moving all associated patients to a selected carrier and deleting the duplicate\. This feature
column2: enabled via the **MergeInsCompany** flag in practice settings
column4: ensuring clean and accurate insurance data\.

column2: when users attempt to respond to stale requests

column4: often opening a support ticket to assist\.

column1: Archive refill requests older than 30 days that have not been responded so that the requests do not appear in Rx Requests queue or patient's Requiring Actions queue\.

I’ve found in my recent explorations of mindreader patterns and datagrid filters that minor tweaks to consumer implementations of MR/S \(mindreader/select\) and challenging to do quickly but have a large impact on the user experience\. Mindreader in particular is a poorly understood component and pattern throughout the org
column2: which I have evidence of holding back designers from making the best UIs possible\. Further
column3: even after a MR/S is implemented
column4: it’s non-trivial to adjust the pattern due to poor documentation and exemplars\. Granted that most of the consumer challenges here are MR-related but that is exacerbated with MR having a different API from Select\. My hypothesis is that if we succeed in this work

column1: 1. Be fluent in their MR/S options and how small changes in the microinteraction pattern can have outsized effects on the UX\.
2. Be better able to pivot their implementations during iterative design\. *We’ll need to infer this
column2: I don’t know that it’s possible to observe or measure this enough to create a meaningful KR\.*

column1: 3. Be more free in selecting MRs as a preferred interaction pattern\. I believe that MR is mistakenly categorized as only for complex inputs
column2: like a CPT code MR\.

column1: ### Goal: It’s easy to for engineering teams to switch between MR/S variants
- Select3 and Mindreader3 have the same API\.
- MultiSelect3 and MultiSelectMindreader3 have the same API\.
- Docs show how to do this and why it’s possible\.
- KR: Count in time steps before and after\.

column2: Engineering
column3: and Product can easily understand and communicate about MR/S variants\. There is a shared language\. It’s easy to know which patterns are likely to be best for a given experience scenario\.

column1: - Interactive demos for all MR/S variants exist in docusaurus
column2: in a centralized location for comparison\. Demos are built using a rich
column3: complex dataset to simulate real-world usage\.

column2: MultiSelect3

column1: - All demos have robust code exemplars\.
- All demos have robust Figma exemplars\.
- How is the end user experience worse today and how would it be better after? What’s the “cost” of having the wrong input element? Reframe in end-user terms\. Main OKR

Providers use templates in Elation to structure their documentation and as an agenda for their visit\. In many cases
column2: templates represent a form of questions that the provider will ask the patient and a space for the provider to fill in their answers\.
the scribe is unaware that templates are present in the visit note** and cannot populate the template based on the conversation between the provider and patient\.

the AI scribe’s output is added to the note as additional text in addition to the provider’s template\.** Providers then copy and paste parts of the AI scribe\-generated output that they find relevant to populate their templates\. Providers then have to manually clean up the note to remove irrelevant parts of the generated note and end up with a satisfactory note\.

column1: Because **providers have to incorporate the AI scribe\-generated output manually** and manually delete parts to reach a satisfactory note
column2: they feel that the AI scribe is not as useful and increases their documentation burden\.

we are exploring using the scribe to populate the provider’s templates when a provider adds a template to their visit note\. We believe this will reduce the frequency of providers needing to edit or delete content in the note \(`reduce intervention rate`\) and improve their satisfaction with the Elation AI Scribe product \(`need a proxy metric here`\)\.

27045664;9414dfa0-f7d7-4c8f-b957-655152521b32;AI Scribe Frontend Observability & Resiliency;AI Scribe is a complicated frontend feature\. It’s not the typical CRUD frontend\. When issues are reported to us
column2: figuring out what’s going on is a very complicated process\. Often times
column3: there’s just no information to go on because the logic is mostly client\-side\.

column1: It’s also a surface that has a very real potential to negatively impact customer trust by potentially “losing encounters”\. Imagine the scenario where a provider is now relying on transcription instead of taking notes during an encounter
column2: and they lose connectivity a couple minutes into the encounter\. The transcription will effectively include nothing\.

column1: We need to increase the observability of this frontend system to make future troubleshooting easier \(and in many cases
column2: possible\)\. The improved observability will also inform how we can make the system more reliable and resilient as well\.

column1: Improved resiliency on the client\-side will most likely take the form of buffering audio recordings on the client and performing automatic connection retrying of the underlying Nabla websocket\.

27579495;8414baea-01e0-44a3-ad99-0e616e595322;Structured Data Capture - Provider Experience;Milestone demo video uploaded as attachment
## 🔗 Key Links: [PRD: Structured Data Capture \- Provider Experience](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/x/RwC3rw)
Providers currently struggle to efficiently document structured data at the point of care without increasing their documentation burden\.
The existing solutions for capturing structured data in Elation are disconnected from their workflows
column2: often requiring providers to access other tools
column4: data documented in VN1 is primarily free text
column5: resulting in the loss of its structure
column6: impacting data extraction
column7: and leading to significant data integrity issues downstream\.

column1: Providers can document structured data using structured inputs instead of free text in VN2\. This feature seamlessly integrates into the note within Elation
column2: enabling providers to stay focused and document efficiently and consistently\. The structured data maintains its integrity
column3: ensuring reliability and easy extraction for downstream use\.

27963996;2ffcd758-8240-498d-bd20-d06716b3f087;Closure Compiler removal;- Appetite: 4 eng\. weeks
- Automatically migrate soy templates to jsx\.
- Automatically migrate closure compiler namespaces to TypeScript + modules
- Replace the Closure Compiler with TypeScript + ESBuild \(or webpack\)

column2: the primary goal is to make it easy to work with legacy and consolidate the number of build tools\. This isn't an effort to make legacy easier because we want to write more legacy
column3: but because legacy being hard to work with also makes it harder to understand the code and reactify it\. Being able to make small legacy changes without losing an entire day is an added benefit\.

column1: - Consolidating the build tooling is also a big chance to improve the DX for the local dev feedback loop and for CI pipeline times \(we can shave off 1-2m here\)\.

column2: the same build tool
column3: and the same module system also gives us the ability to reuse code between these codebases\.

column1: ### Legacy JavaScript → TypeScript Stretch Goal Transformations
In addition to *just* transforming the legacy code into side-effect style imports that will work with a modern build toolchain
column2: there’s another set of transformations that we can consider\. These vary in complexity and should not be considered part of the MVP
column3: as they are not required to make the build toolchain work
column4: but do improve the codebase\. These make decent candidates for exploring during the hackathon:

column1: - **Canonical Forms**\. Identifying some common constructs simplifying the multiple ways of expressing that construct to a single canonical form\.

- ES6 Classes\. There’s a handful of ways for developers to define “classes” going back 13 years\. Whether that’s your lib’s \.class method or using prototype\.methods\. All of these constructs should be simplified to using ES6 classes\.

- Getters and Setters\. The legacy codebase did some *interesting* things to the base Function prototype\. Like adding functionality for defining class properties that automatically get dynamically generated getters and setters \(additional fields on the prototype with `get` and `set` prefixes\.\)\. These can be transformed to *actual* getters and setters on ES6 classes\. It would also require that call-sites are also updated to the new syntax though\.
- The existing dynamically generated getters and setters are a problem because they make it impossible for your IDE to provide any static hints to you
column2: like following references or go-to definition\. That means they’re also a problem if you want to ultimately type-check the whole codebase\.

column1: - **Side Effect Imports → Actual Modules through Dependency Graph analysis\.** After the initial transformation
column2: all the code should function in the same way at runtime
column3: that is everything is bound to a namespace on the el8 global object\. Some of these portions of the codebase will naturally lend themselves well to being turned directly into modules
column4: that is specifically code that doesn’t get used by the react codebase at runtime\.

column1: - **Bootstrapping TypeScript Types**\. Through a combination of static analysis and sampling runtime values we can derive a simple set of Types for some of the codebase\. It will not be exhaustive
column2: but it will at least be a starting point for reducing the number of untyped files we have in our frontend codebase\.

column1: - Single view/templating language for frontend development
- Consistent \(or at least modern\) build tooling across the frontend\.
- Go-to definition and follow references in modern IDEs
- Improved CI times by extracting legacy builds from the docker image build \(can be run in parallel
column2: and doesn’t have to block subsequent steps like the build\_arm64 job does\)\.

Elation’s current webhook implementation is heavily used and imposes a fairly large amount of DB traffic\. If we reduce the load on the Hippo DB we improve our cost\-to\-serve metrics
column2: enhance overall DB health and unlock a pathway towards a more scalable
the current webhook implementation doesn’t provide strong guarantees about the order of event delivery in the event of errors \(or that failed webhook requests will be retried at all\!\)\.

column1: An Event\-streaming based webhook implementation offers a number of enhancements:
- No longer depending on Django signals to detect changes
column2: making it possible to move webhook processing out of the Hippo monolith\. We have patterns in place for generating event\-streaming events out of microservices
column3: allowing non\-hippo components to more easily add webhook support\.

column1: - No need to hydrate the entity as part of webhook processing
column2: event\-streaming provides a fully hydrated APIv2 model with a strongly predictable delivery latency \([sub 3s](https://el8.grafana.net/d/e5a2d2ef-8c4c-4073-bd69-733c9dfc1386/hydrator-transit-latency?orgId=1)\)
column3: this gives us more predictable webhook processing performance\.

column1: - Removal of the DB write traffic caused by the current Webhook implementation and at least a significant reduction \(if not the complete removal of\) the read traffic currently associated with webhooks\.
- An opportunity to re\-evaluate our error handling and event re\-drive processes for webhooks
column2: by making use of DLQs and retry counters
column3: we can improve visibility into the status and functioning of our webhooks\.

column1: Some tech notes from previous meetings:
- It uses session cookies to authenticate
- Session state is stored in redis in the shared microservices redis cluster
- The [authentication service](https://github.com/elationemr/authentication-service)is responsible for validating the session cookie and injecting the internal token
- More details can be found in the original [ADR](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/x/AYCGk)
- Need for more granular control and management options

column2: compliance

column1: - Extra work for customers in monitoring and managing configurations
- Concerns about giving full admin privileges to users
- Elation’s current approach to settings management is governed by the admin / non\-admin paradigm\. If a user is not an admin
column2: they can see and manage some settings; if a user is an admin
column3: they can see and manage all settings\. While this may work for smaller organizations
column5: distributed organizations need a way to empower local users to make day\-to\-day changes
column6: while ensuring their access is limited so they don’t mistakenly make global changes that impact the entire organization\. Currently
column7: customers must either centralize all administrative functions in an abundance of caution that reduces administrative mistakes but decreases efficiency
column8: or they can provide global admin rights more broadly but risk administrative error\.

column1: - If organizations can empower local users to manage some settings without giving them global access rights
column2: then organizations will be able to confidently scale
column3: balancing local administrative efficiency while minimizing costly administrative errors\.

column1: - View summary of roles and permissions in Account Details

column2: there is no streamlined way for practices to manage CPT and diagnosis code mappings for lab tests

column1: **Solution:** The Lab Coding Settings page will allow practices to manage the CPT and diagnosis code mappings specifically for lab tests\.

column2: paving the way for an upcoming coding automation project\.

28978235;92bebc32-73e8-4204-a6b6-980a28c7de2c;Note Assist: Draft Lab orders on behalf of providers;[PRD](https://elationhealth.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/PROD/pages/2685043847/PRD+-+AI+Actions+wip)
Framing: AI Actions enables us to achieve the dream of eliminating the administrative burden of documentation for providers\. With AI Actions
column2: providers focus on making clinical decisions
column3: while the system intelligently carries out the necessary steps to get the clinical decision in a state where it can be executed\. The provider then reviews and signs off on the draft\.

column1: AI Actions presents an opportunity to significantly reduce the time providers spend on administrative steps required to capture their clinical intent and execute on the task by offloading the order drafting \(related functional job\) to an LLM during a patient\-provider encounter \(core functional job\)\.
1. Provider makes clinical decision to prescribe a specific medication for a patient during an encounter
1. Provider communicates the decision to the patient
1. Provider makes clinical decision to prescribe a specific medication for a patient during an encounter
1. Provider communicates the decision to the patient
1. LLM ambiently captures the order and creates a draft prescription\/fills out the form on behalf of the provider
1. Provider opens prescription form and reviews
- eliminate the intermediate steps that a provider has to take to translate their clinical decision into action

- eliminate the administrative burden that is created by those intermediate steps – speed up the time to complete documentation by automating as much of the space between clinical decision making and action\/fulfillment of those decisions\.
LLMs give us the ability to translate unstructured text into structured data\. That gives us an opportunity to extract unstructured text related to order placement
column2: and ***eliminate the clerical steps of translating clinical decision to actions*** by filling out the form\/drafting orders on their behalf\.
we would eventually want this functionality to support ALL order types \(which is where I believe there will be lots of complexity\)\.

column5: structuring the information according to internal API requirements and performing necessary lookups of coded medical context

column2: particularly those with recurring memberships
column3: are looking for a lower\-cost payment option\. Our current checkout only supports credit and debit cards

column6: which offers ACH payments\. Without ACH support
column7: we risk losing customers who need a more cost\-effective and seamless way to collect recurring payments\.

column1: By integrating ACH payments with Stripe

column3: reducing transaction fees compared to card payments\. ACH provides a secure and reliable way to collect recurring membership payments while lowering costs for practices\. This option will help retain customers
column5: and expand payment flexibility for both practices and patients\.

column1: As practices look to optimize their revenue and reduce overhead
column2: offering a lower\-cost payment method is critical\. ACH adoption is growing

column5: and strengthen our position as the preferred billing solution for membership\-based care\.

column1: 29350896;94f222d1-283e-4b28-9c91-79af28eaef3b;SDC - Stronger Chart Access Controls / Ability to prevent breaking the glass when unauthorized user accesses cohort / VIP;"Background
- Customers need to restrict access to patient charts for a variety of different reasons:
- Customers may wish to restrict access to charts with sensitive data \(reproductive health
column2: mental / behavioral health

column1: - Customers may wish to grant access to select charts to 3rd parties for auditing and other use cases
- Customers may not want non-provider users to access patient charts
- Elation offers premium customers a few ways to restrict access to charts currently:
- [Chart Access Permission Control](https://help.elationemr.com/s/article/User-Accounts-Guide-Chart-access-permission-options) - Determine if an individual user is able to access any charts
- [VIP Charts](https://help.elationemr.com/s/article/vip-chart-feature) - Configure a chart so only the assigned provider is authorized to access it

- [Patient Cohorts](https://help.elationemr.com/s/article/Patient-Chart-Guide-Restricting-access-to-patient-charts-based-on-patient-criteria)- Define a set of patients based on filter criteria and limit access to one or more user groups
- VIP charts and patient cohorts both offer 'break the glass' options for unauthorized users to access a chart
- This enables users other than the assigned provider or users associated with a cohort to access a chart when needed
column2: such as during an emergency

column1: - The access is logged in either the chronological record \(VIP\) or in the patient cohort access logs \(patient cohorts\)
- Customers do not currently have a way to fully prevent unauthorized access to patient charts
- This creates data security risks as unauthorized users can access sensitive information by breaking the glass
- Provide a way for customers to prevent unauthorized patient chart access
- Ideally this is something that practices can choose to use\. Customers that wish to still allow emergency access \(""break the glass""\) can keep the standard settings
column2: while those who want to fully prevent unauthorized access can enable this option

column1: - This has been an issue for a long time
column2: but it has been getting additional focus from customers who are offering mental and behavioral health services\. Additionally
column3: this is a greater concern as there is new legislation around protecting reproductive health data

column1: - Many customers in Elation's medium group and Innovator cohort have specifically requested these capabilities\. These customers represent \~$3M in ARR
- Elation currently offers two methods to restrict access to patient charts:

column2: which limits access to the assigned provider

column1: - Including the chart in a patient cohort
column2: which limits access to the user groups assigned to that cohort

column1: - While these tools are meant to restrict access to a patient chart
column2: both options can be bypassed through ‘break-the-glass’ functionality\. This capability ensures unauthorized users can access a chart in an emergency\. While this capability improves patient safety and is supported by auditing capabilities
column3: it does not provide the full chart access control that some organizations are seeking\. This stronger chart access control is particularly important around sensitive health data \(ex: reproductive health

column1: - If organizations have a way to fully restrict access to sensitive patient charts
column2: they will reduce the risk of patient data privacy violations

column1: - Stronger Chart Access Control \(toggle preventing unauthorized access of VIP charts and charts in a patient cohort\)

29374321;5bdb01ec-696c-46d9-8ef0-ed183838abd4;Note Assist: ICD-10 Coding (existing problems);## Current State
We have an opportunity to better align Note Assist with the realities of longitudinal care
column2: which is essential in primary care settings\.
when clinicians document the assessment and plan for a patient’s existing problems
column4: separate entry rather than referencing the existing problem from the patient’s problem list—curated by the clinician\. This results in a disconnect that impacts continuity of care documentation\.

column1: This disconnect causes two primary issues:
- When clinicians track changes to a problem
column2: the newly added entry is not linked to the original problem in the patient’s problem list
column3: breaking the continuity essential to long\-term care\.

column1: - The newly added version often lacks the specificity and associated ICD\-10 code of the original
column2: increasing the documentation burden for clinicians as they manually correct these entries\.
Note Assist will

column1: - **Reference existing problems** from the patient’s problem list curated by the clinician when generating the assessment and plan\. If a problem already exists in the problem list
column2: Note Assist will incorporate it directly into the note along with its ICD\-10 code

column1: - **New Problems**: For new problems that are not present in the problem list
column2: Note Assist will display the ICD description only
column3: without suggesting an ICD\-10 code\. Adding ICD\-10 codes for new problems will be considered in a future release\.

column2: maintain documentation continuity
column3: and reduce the need for manual corrections
column4: making Note Assist more aligned with the requirements of longitudinal care\.

29459053;2e090e5a-cc58-4656-9eda-fdabe3d09807;Note Assist: Fact based Pipeline, till GA;**What is it**
- A new approach to our prompting pipeline that atomizes each section of the note
column2: and allows us to generate them independent of each other\.

column1: - The key benefit of this approach is that we are able to make changes to specific section of the note
column2: without running the risk that a change to one section negatively impacts another\. For example
column3: changes to HPI in the past have led to negative impacts to Assessment and Plan\.

column1: - This also makes it easier for us to add new sections to the note
column2: such as ROS
column3: without risking performance or accuracy of the rest of the note\.

column1: We currently have a use case
column2: Task scheduler
column3: that we have identified would benefit from publishing to Kafka directly

column2: Hippo

High quality transcripts are critical to the accuracy of Note Assist\. When the transcription quality is degraded
column2: we run the risk of generating notes that don’t truly capture what happened during a patient encounter\. This creates risks in clinical care by introducing several issues:

column1: - **Incorrect Diarization:** The system sometimes mixes up who is speaking
column2: blurring the line between provider and patient dialogue\.

column1: - **Incorrect Segmentation:** Conversations can be split at the wrong points
column2: distorting the intended context\.

column1: - **Transcription Errors:** Mistakes—like misspelling medication names or getting numerical details \(vitals
column2: dosages\) wrong—undermine the reliability of the transcript\.

column1: Each of these issues directly impacts the accuracy of our notes
column2: potentially leading to misinterpretations and errors in patient care\.
we are working to incorporate the Diarization feature from our speech\-to\-text service into the Soapgen engine\. Our hypothesis is that enabling diarization will improve segmentation
column3: which will improve the quality of our transcripts and resulting notes\.

column1: This work doesn't offer any new solutions to speaker identification; this step will continue as is in the soapgen engine\.

column1: Our objective is to grow AI attach rate\* on Elation from ~5% to 30\+% of providers in the next 12 months\.

Closed lost data consistently shows that pricing is a key reason prospects choose not to convert\. Many report finding similar products at half the price and perceive those lower\-cost alternatives as offering comparable value to ours\.
Our strategy is not to compete on price but to deliver differentiated value that aligns with our long\-term vision of an AI\-native EHR\. As we establish ourselves in the market
column2: this feedback suggests that:

column1: 1. Our current differentiation may not be strong enough to create the necessary distance between us and our competitors\.
1. The challenge isn’t a reluctance to pay more for meaningful differentiation but rather a perceived gap between our product’s value and price\.

column2: we are committed to consistently innovating and enhancing our product's unique value\. By doing so
column3: we will decisively shift market perception
column4: driving more prospects to choose our solution over competitors
column5: all while maintaining our existing pricing\.

column1: *\*Attach rate here combines both existing and net\-new business and means paid adoption\.*

column2: facilitates action
column3: and ensures they get credit for their work\.

Based on feedback from our CGM team
column2: we've identified interest in extending Note Assist access to staff users
column3: beyond just providers\. The CGM team [estimates that in approximately 30% of their deals
column4: they could have sold at least one additional staff license](https://elation.slack.com/archives/C05CTFXD5RC/p1731088128817139?thread_ts=1729623284.464359&cid=C05CTFXD5RC)\. This insight presents a compelling opportunity to explore how Note Assist might support staff workflows in practices and potentially drive further adoption and additional revenue\.

column2: we have now made it possible for practices to assign Note Assist seats to their staff users\. **This will allow us to run a focused test to validate the true demand for staff licenses\.** Our key objectives are:

column1: 1. **Assess Willingness to Pay**: Determine if practices are willing to pay $199 per staff seat\.
1. **Understand Use Cases**: Gain insight into the specific use cases that staff members have for Note Assist\.
1. **Identify Barriers**: Identify any additional questions or barriers that need to be addressed before considering a broader rollout\.
We will focus on practices that have been using Note Assist for some time
column2: as their experiences will provide valuable insights into whether they are eager to make it available to staff\. Understanding how Note Assist might be integrated into broader practice workflows is key to our evaluation\.

column1: This test is exclusively for existing paying customers that the CGM team believes they could have sold staff seats to \(~35 practices\)\. We will need an explicit list of practices and the signal that leads us to believe these practices have a propensity to purchase staff seats documented in order to include them in the test\.
The next step is for CGMs to reach out to the practices they previously identified as having potential interest in staff seats\. We propose the following:
1. **Outreach to Practices**: CGMs will contact these practices and inform them of the new availability of Note Assist for staff users\.
1. **Two\-Week Trial**: Offer a two\-week trial for staff users to these practices\. This trial period will serve three key purposes:
1. **Evaluate Staff Engagement**: Gain insight into how staff members engage with Note Assist and the value they derive from it\.
1. **Practice Evaluation**: Allow practices to assess whether Note Assist can effectively support their staff workflows in its current form\.
See which practices make a purchasing decision
column2: and the reasons behind the decision\.

30844129;1ac20cba-3d9b-4413-b80d-a30558161257;Auto-chart creation;**Problem statement:** System\-generated charts create data security risks
column2: duplicate chart management challenges
column4: making it harder for practices to maintain accurate and compliant patient records\.

column1: **Solution hypothesis: IF** the system provides a way for practices to view all auto created charts as soon **as they are created
column2: ** THEN users can mange these in order to ensure they have one source of truth for patient care\.

column1: Charts can be generated without a customer's knowledge or action through a few different [methods](https://help.elationemr.com/s/article/merging-duplicate-charts):
- Incoming direct messages for patients not in the practice
- Incoming lab results for patients not in the practice
- Incoming prescription requests for patients not in the practice
- An integration triggered the creation of the chart
- Self\-scheduled appointments from patients who are not in the practice
column2: or patients who entered information that doesn't match what is on file

column1: Customers don't have an easy way to know when a chart has been created automatically
- The UI doesn't notify the customer when one of these charts are created
- Customers often only discover these charts when they search for a patient and see multiple matching records
column2: or if there is an item in their queue associated with one of these charts

column1: - When a customer opens one of these charts
column2: they'll see a banner indicating ""This chart was created automatically and may be a duplicate\."" with the option to merge the chart with another existing chart

column1: - It appears that dismissing the banner causes it to not show up anymore for other users
This quiet creation of charts can be problematic for a few reasons:
1. **Data security risks:** practices are responsible for safeguarding the data of their patients\. When non\-patient data is entered into their system without their knowledge
column2: they can be held liable for not properly handling that data

column1: 2. **Duplicate chart management:** when system generated charts exist
column2: it can be difficult for practices to select the correct chart when trying to pull up a patient record\. This can result in information being entered in the wrong record
column3: or the wrong record being referenced

column1: 3. **Difficulty closing the loop:** if a chart was created in error
column2: such as a pharmacy or lab sending a request or results to the wrong practice
column3: it can be difficult for a provider to take the appropriate correction steps if they're not aware of the action

30965701;b38bfb8f-e5f5-4a9c-8c4e-01e7406ddd5b;EHR as source of truth for Patient Insurance;**Problem**
Elation Billing \(EB\) and the EHR currently operate with a one\-way sync for insurance data
column2: allowing edits in EB that do not reflect back in the EHR\. This creates **data drift**
column3: where discrepancies between the two systems lead to claim denials
column5: and inefficiencies for practices\. Without a reliable source of truth for insurance information
column6: staff must manually reconcile differences
column7: increasing administrative burden and the risk of revenue loss due to incorrect payer details or outdated coverage information\.

column1: By supporting the **EHR’s insurance versioning capability** and making the **EHR the source of truth** for insurance data
column2: we eliminate inconsistencies between the two systems\. This ensures that any updates made in the EHR—such as policy changes
column4: or corrections—are accurately reflected in EB
column5: preventing billing mismatches and claim denials\. Implementing a **bidirectional sync or enforcement of EHR\-driven insurance data** will streamline workflows
column6: improve billing accuracy
column7: and reduce administrative overhead for practices\.

column1: The **FOPE team is planning to GA the EHR insurance versioning capability in Q2**
column2: making this the ideal time to align our updates in Elation Billing\. With more practices relying on EB for insurance claims management
column3: ensuring data integrity is critical to customer satisfaction and retention\. Insurance\-taking practices are a **key growth segment**
column4: and failing to address this data drift could drive frustration
column6: and potential churn\. Additionally
column7: our competitors offer more integrated billing workflows
column8: and resolving this issue positions Elation Billing as a **best\-in\-class** solution\. As we enhance other billing functionalities like ERA processing and denial management
column9: now is the time to address this foundational issue to improve efficiency
column11: and reinforce the EHR as the **single source of truth** for practice operations\.
